in_practice_history.piece_title: Expectations for an Engineering Manager at Monzo
in_practice_history.author: Diogo Matias

queries_run: engineering manager calibration skip-level managing managers May 2026
engineering team layoffs restructure reorg engineering org May 2026

Intuit layoffs 3000 AI refocus engineering date May 2026
tech layoffs restructure week of May 25 2026

candidates.headline: Layoff wave continues through the window — Webflow and Groupon cut staff May 27, sustaining the 'restructure around AI' pattern that defined Meta, Intuit and Coinbase
candidates.summary: At least 4,550 US tech employees were laid off or scheduled for layoffs in the week ending 27 May 2026, with Groupon cutting ~400 (a quarter of staff), Webflow announcing abrupt cuts, Lightricks preparing AI-driven reductions, and NetApp cutting 77 — a continuation of the larger May wave (Meta ~8,000 announced 20 May, Intuit ~3,000 announced 20 May, Coinbase ~700 / 14% announced 5 May). The common driver across the wave is restructuring around AI and flattening management layers.
candidates.so_what: The pattern — not any single company — is the leadership signal: org charts are being flattened and management layers thinned as the default response to AI, which reshapes levelling, span-of-control and EM career-path conversations.
candidates.chartable_data: Layoff counts by company across the May wave (Meta 8000, Intuit 3000, Coinbase 700, Groupon 400, NetApp 77) — bar chart or timeline of the month's cuts
candidates.notes: Meta layoffs were the Issue 11 Lead and CANNOT re-anchor. The fresh, in-window angle is the continuation (Webflow/Groupon 27 May) plus the cross-company STRUCTURAL pattern (flattening layers), not Meta specifically. Treat as the week's-pattern story, not a Meta retread.

candidates.headline: Coinbase's 'no pure managers' restructure — player-coaches and one-person AI-native pods — names a concrete new org shape
candidates.summary: Coinbase cut ~14% of staff (700 roles) and announced it will have 'no pure managers,' replacing them with 'player-coaches' who manage while also contributing, and capping the org at five layers between execs and its ~4,300 remaining staff. CEO Brian Armstrong also floated 'AI-native pods,' potentially one-person teams directing agents across eng/design/PM roles.
candidates.so_what: It is the most explicit articulation yet of the post-AI management shape — the player-coach model and layer-capping directly change levelling, the IC-vs-management track, and what 'manager' means.
candidates.notes: Original event 5 May — just before the window. Eligible only via the new-in-window traction exception (the layoff wave continuing through 27 May keeps it live); if used, must state the original announcement date. Strongest single articulation of the management-shape shift; pairs with the wave story.

candidates.headline: 'Demand for managers is up, expectations have changed again' — the EM role is being rewritten around teaching and AI-stakeholder management
candidates.source_name: Diary of an Engineering Manager (Suresh Choudhary) / EMDiary
candidates.summary: An EM-practitioner essay arguing expectations on engineering managers have shifted again for 2026: a more teaching-oriented approach (e.g. structured daily 30-minute 1:1s to coach specific skills), heavier stakeholder-expectation management around AI, and a reality that some strong managers of the prior era are no longer considered good now. Frames the middle-management squeeze created by the layoff wave.
candidates.so_what: Gives managers-of-managers the vocabulary for the recalibration their EMs are living through — useful as context alongside the harder layoff-wave news.
candidates.notes: Essay, not news. Borderline date. Better as Other News / context inside a management QT than an anchor.

candidates.headline: GitHub Copilot scraps flat pricing for usage-based AI Credits on 1 June — agentic usage now metered, and heavy-agent teams will pay more
candidates.summary: From 1 June 2026 GitHub Copilot replaces Premium Request Units with token-metered 'GitHub AI Credits.' Seat prices hold (Business $19, Enterprise $39, each including matching monthly credits) and completions stay unlimited, but every agentic feature is now measured and billed by token consumption at per-model API rates — so teams with intense agent usage will see bills rise. GitHub also quietly paused new Pro/Pro+/Student sign-ups in late April.
candidates.so_what: The cutover lands inside this window and converts a fixed line-item into a variable, usage-driven one — forcing an AI-spend / finance-budget conversation engineering leaders must have now, not later.
candidates.chartable_data: Before/after pricing model (flat seat + PRU vs seat + metered AI Credits); compare-table of Business vs Enterprise included credits
candidates.notes: Announced/updated 12 May but the EFFECTIVE cutover is 1 June — squarely a 'decide/act this week' budget trigger inside the window. Classic v3.4 'tool pricing change that triggers a finance conversation' = Ways of working, not At a Glance. Strong Lead contender on the operational-decision test.

candidates.headline: 'The harness is the product' — Anthropic, OpenAI, Google and Microsoft split on how to PRICE the agent runtime, not the model
candidates.summary: An analysis arguing the competitive battleground has moved from model weights to the 'harness' — the runtime that manages state, retries and tool hygiene — and the four big labs now price it incompatibly: Anthropic charges $0.08/session-hour for its Managed Agents runtime, OpenAI ships a model-native harness in its open-source Agents SDK with no first-party runtime fee, and Google/Microsoft bundle priced primitives inside broader platforms. The cost model determines lock-in.
candidates.so_what: Whichever harness a team standardises on now sets its multi-year AI cost structure and switching cost — a build-vs-buy / vendor-standardisation decision leaders own, framed as ways of working rather than a tool spec.
candidates.chartable_data: Compare-table: four vendors x pricing model for the agent runtime (Anthropic session-hour, OpenAI no-runtime-fee, Google/MS bundled primitives)
candidates.notes: In-window (26 May) and reads as analysis/essay more than a discrete launch — strong QT/analysis anchor or feeds the Copilot-pricing Lead as the 'why this is industry-wide' context. event_or_essay = essay, so cannot be the Lead but qualifies as the 3rd anchor (non-event) if needed.

candidates.headline: Anthropic buys SDK toolchain Stainless for $300M+ and will wind down the hosted generator OpenAI, Google and Cloudflare all relied on
candidates.summary: Anthropic acquired Stainless — whose AI turns OpenAPI specs into idiomatic, typed SDKs across languages — for over $300M (its fourth acquisition in six months after Bun, Vercept, Coefficient Bio). Stainless's customers include OpenAI, Google, Cloudflare and Meta; Anthropic will wind down the hosted SDK generator, though existing customers keep the SDKs already generated. Read as Anthropic vertically integrating the agent stack from runtime to connectivity layer.
candidates.so_what: Teams whose API-client SDKs were generated on a now-sunsetting hosted tool face a real migration/ownership question, and the broader move concentrates the developer-tooling layer under one vendor.
candidates.notes: Event date 18 May — just outside the window's start. Coverage and analysis (New Stack, multiple outlets) ran through 19-26 May, giving in-window traction. Good At a Glance / Other News item; supports the 'tooling-layer concentration' throughline with the harness story.

candidates.headline: New DORA report: strong engineering foundations — not the AI tools — drive the ROI of AI-assisted development
candidates.source_name: InfoQ (on the 2026 DORA 'ROI of AI-Assisted Software Development' report)
candidates.summary: InfoQ covers the DORA team's follow-up report finding AI returns come from the underlying organisational system, not the tools: AI is a multiplier of existing conditions (helping strong teams, exposing weak ones). DORA lead Nathen Harvey says the greatest returns come from 'a strategic focus on the underlying organizational system,' and without it AI creates 'localized pockets of productivity ... lost in downstream chaos.' Cited Stanford data: 35-40% gains on greenfield, often <=10% on complex legacy.
candidates.so_what: Gives leaders an evidence-based frame for sequencing AI rollouts — fix delivery foundations first — and a counter to vendor productivity claims.
candidates.chartable_data: AI productivity gain by task type (greenfield 35-40% vs legacy <=10%); amplifier effect on high- vs low-performing teams
candidates.notes: CAUTION: DORA's J-Curve was the Issue 11 Lead and DORA cannot be re-anchored as a featured anchor per the no-repeat rule (v1.31). This is a DIFFERENT DORA report (ROI of AI, published in-window 20 May) but curation must treat DORA-as-source carefully — likely Other News / On the Radar, or a single-sentence cross-issue throughline rather than a fresh anchor.

candidates.headline: incident.io reframes on-call reliability around the customer, not the component — 'good minutes / total minutes' and dual-provider redundancy
candidates.summary: incident.io published (28 May) how it measures on-call reliability from the customer's outcome rather than internal components: a 'good minutes / total minutes' SLO (minutes with <10% error rate count as functional, so self-healing blips don't count as outages), 99.99% monthly targets for alert ingestion and <5-min notification latency, dual telecom providers so a Twilio/APNs degradation isn't an excuse, and subtracting customer-configured delays from latency math. Near-perfect over 12 months bar one Oct 2025 AWS-driven outage.
candidates.so_what: A concrete, transferable model for how to define and measure reliability that a leader can bring to an SLO / on-call design conversation without running the system.
candidates.chartable_data: SLO targets (99.99% availability, <5 min latency); 'good minutes/total minutes' methodology as a process diagram
candidates.notes: In-window (28 May). Strongest fit as a Ways-of-working / Operating-at-scale In Practice or a craft quick take; named-org but the author byline is only a first name ('Mike') — verify full authorship before using as In Practice (the named-operator quality is borderline).

candidates.headline: Microsoft Defender zero-days (RedSun / UnDefend) under active exploitation — CISA-flagged, patches rolling out
candidates.summary: Microsoft began rolling out patches for two Defender zero-days exploited in the wild, with CISA confirming active exploitation: CVE-2026-41091 ('RedSun', a local privilege-escalation flaw) and CVE-2026-45498 ('UnDefend', which lets standard users block Defender definition updates). Part of a busy late-May exploitation cycle that also includes an Exchange Server zero-day (CVE-2026-42897) and the earlier cPanel mass-exploitation (CVE-2026-41940, CVSS 9.8).
candidates.so_what: For Windows/Defender-dependent orgs this is an act-this-week awareness-and-delegate item — the leader's job is to confirm patching is owned, not to remediate.
candidates.notes: Per the audience-fit gate this is a Week-at-a-Glance / Action Required one-liner, NOT an anchor — the useful takeaway requires running the system. Date in-window (26 May).

candidates.headline: AWS US-EAST-1 thermal event post-mortems land — single-hall cooling failure physically damaged EC2/EBS, knocked Coinbase offline ~7 hours
candidates.summary: Analysis and DR post-mortems published this week dissect the 7-8 May AWS US-EAST-1 thermal event: a single data-centre hall lost cooling, triggering a power loss that physically damaged EC2 instances and EBS volumes on affected racks and causing multi-hour outages at Coinbase (~7 hours), FanDuel and CME Group. The follow-on coverage focuses on cross-region disaster-recovery lessons.
candidates.so_what: A live case study in single-region concentration risk and DR design — the kind of outage analysis that anchors a 'where is our single point of failure' conversation.
candidates.chartable_data: Outage durations by affected company; single-hall failure -> cascade as a process/dependency diagram
candidates.notes: Original outage 7-8 May (outside window); the DR post-mortem coverage is in-window (traction exception applies — must state the original event date if used). Good QT or Other News on disaster-recovery framing.

candidates.headline: Railway publishes full post-mortem on the 19 May GCP account-suspension outage — control-plane dependency cascaded beyond Google Cloud
candidates.summary: Railway's incident report (published this week) details the ~8-hour platform-wide outage after Google Cloud wrongly suspended its production account on 19-20 May. Workloads on Railway's own Metal and AWS burst environments stayed up, but its edge proxies depend on a GCP-hosted control-plane API for routing tables, so as route caches expired the outage cascaded beyond GCP itself.
candidates.so_what: A sharp lesson in hidden control-plane dependencies — a system can be 'multi-cloud' on paper yet still single-point-fail through a shared control plane.
candidates.chartable_data: Architecture: control-plane dependency causing cascade despite redundant data-plane (diagram)
candidates.notes: CAUTION: the Railway-GCP outage was an Issue 11 anchor and CANNOT re-anchor (v1.31 no-repeat). The post-mortem published in-window (22 May) is genuinely new material, but per the rule it can only be a single-sentence cross-issue throughline, not a featured anchor. Captured for completeness.

candidates.headline: The banking AI bifurcation: incumbents run AI as a cost engine (15,000 jobs cut, $47bn profit) while fintechs build AI-native operating models
candidates.summary: Analysis (updated through 29 May) arguing a structural split in banking AI: incumbents use AI for cost takeout — the six largest US banks cut ~15,000 jobs in Q1 while posting $47bn combined profit; JPMorgan runs '500 AI use cases in production' on a $2bn AI budget; Goldman's CEO says AI does '95% of the work on IPO prospectuses'; BofA says AI saved ~2,000 FTEs. Fintechs instead build AI-native, API-first operating models with superior unit economics (Mercury ~$812.5k revenue/employee; Ramp shipped 300+ AI product innovations in 2025; Block cut 40% headcount yet grew gross profit 27%).
candidates.so_what: It is exactly the world the reader operates in — frames whether AI investment is positioned as an efficiency engine or a revenue engine, the strategic question their bank customers and fintech peers are deciding now.
candidates.chartable_data: Incumbent vs fintech comparison: revenue/employee, jobs cut, AI use cases, profit growth — strong grouped-bar or compare-table
candidates.notes: Reads as analysis (essay) with a strong dataset, updated through 29 May so in-window via the traction exception. Banking/fintech first-class subject matter per v1.31. Cannot be the Lead (essay) but is a strong fintech QT / Outside-In-adjacent candidate and the best banking-engineering framing this week.

candidates.headline: Stripe Sessions 2026 fallout: Machine Payments Protocol and the agentic-commerce primitives reshape how money moves between agents
candidates.summary: At Sessions 2026 Stripe shipped 288 products, anchored by the Machine Payments Protocol (MPP, co-authored with Tempo) — a spec for agents to transact programmatically (microtransactions, recurring, streaming payments) via the PaymentIntents API — plus upgraded Revenue infrastructure for dimensional pricing and streaming payments, sitting alongside MCP/UCP/x402 in the emerging agent-commerce stack.
candidates.so_what: Defines the rails agentic commerce will run on — directly relevant to a loyalty/commerce-media fintech and the bank customers asking about agent payments.
candidates.notes: BANNED as anchor — Stripe MPP was an Issue 11 anchor (cannot re-anchor, v1.31). Also adjacent-source caution. Captured only as Other News / context; do not feature.

candidates.headline: Visa and Mastercard expand agentic-commerce deployments — Trusted Agent Protocol and Agent Pay move toward mainstream for the 2026 holiday season
candidates.summary: Coverage of Visa and Mastercard scaling their agentic-commerce frameworks: Visa's Trusted Agent Protocol (TAP, launched Oct 2025 with Cloudflare) signs an agent's identity into HTTP headers for merchant verification, and Mastercard's Agent Pay uses Agentic Tokens; both lean on Web Bot Auth to let networks verify legitimate AI shopping agents. Visa expects millions of consumers using AI agents to purchase by the 2026 holiday season.
candidates.so_what: The card networks are standardising how agents authenticate and pay — a loyalty/commerce-media platform's roadmap for agent checkout will be shaped by which protocol wins.
candidates.notes: Underlying protocols predate the window (TAP Oct 2025), but the in-window expansion/deployment coverage (21 May) plus the holiday-season framing gives fresh traction. Solid loyalty/commerce-media fintech QT — and a non-Stripe payments-rail angle to avoid the Stripe re-anchor.

candidates.headline: The Middle East is quietly becoming a top fintech frontier — UAE FIT programme 85% complete, Saudi open finance, regional cloud regions landing
candidates.summary: MEF profiles the ME fintech build-out: the UAE's Financial Infrastructure Transformation (FIT) programme is ~85% complete with open-finance regulation and a unified 2025 banking/fintech law; Saudi (SAMA) is pushing further into open finance atop 261 fintechs (+21% YoY); regional cloud capacity is expanding (Microsoft's Saudi Azure region GA in Q4 2026) enabling data-residency compliance. Open-banking APIs underpin mobile payments, micro-lending and Arabic-native conversational banking.
candidates.so_what: Directly maps the ME bank-engineering landscape the reader's customers operate in — open-finance API mandates and new cloud regions are concrete partnership and integration drivers.
candidates.notes: In-window (14 May). WebFetch returned 403 (anti-scraping) but the URL resolves in search. Industry-overview essay, not a discrete event — best as Other News / On the Radar context for ME readers, not an anchor.

candidates.headline: Brex/Ramp autonomous finance agents cross 1M conversations/month — Capital One's $5.15bn Brex acquisition reshapes the spend-management stack
candidates.summary: Coverage of the spend-management space: Brex and Ramp shipped autonomous finance agents (receipts, approvals, vendor management), with three Fortune 500 deployments crossing 1M conversations/month in April; Ramp's agents block 3.5-8.8% of out-of-policy spend at swipe and auto-code 60% of invoices at 99% precision. Capital One's $5.15bn Brex acquisition (agreed Jan 2026) is expected to close mid-2026, consolidating the corporate-card/fintech stack.
candidates.so_what: Shows production agents with real action authority (moving money, blocking spend) in fintech — the credible end of the agent spectrum, relevant to anyone building agent features into financial products.
candidates.notes: Ramp is BANNED for Outside In (history). Fine as a beat candidate. Useful as a concrete 'production agents with action authority' data point for the agent-spectrum gloss; Other News / context.

leadership_read.headline: A manager's guide to performance calibration — the 'slippery slope' from ratings to stack-ranking, and seven ways to resist it
leadership_read.summary: Karp names a three-stage 'slippery slope of performance calibrations': (1) dissociation/fungibility — people abstracted into ratings; (2) performance curves — false objectivity that ignores how level and tenure shape a 'normal' distribution; (3) stack ranking — performance becomes zero-sum so someone must be at the bottom. He counters with seven recommendations: local calibrations, eliminating curves, including everyone, message focus, trusting managers, defining clear ratings, and analysing demographic trends.
leadership_read.so_what: A manager-of-managers running calibration this cycle gets a concrete framework for keeping the process fair before it drifts toward stack-ranking.
leadership_read.notes: Evergreen (2022) but on-theme for the manager-of-managers season and a non-AI craft read — fits the v1.35 long-form-variety rule. last_leadership_read was Honeycomb, so LeadDev is eligible. Normative how-to, so NOT eligible for In Practice (fails the journey test) but valid as a craft Leadership Read.

leadership_read.headline: Would I still go the engineering-manager route in 2026? — IC/management parity and the harder middle-management squeeze
leadership_read.summary: Ojstersek argues the management path has changed since 2020: the IC track is now standardised and pay has reached parity, so the old financial pull toward management is gone; manager/architect/staff responsibilities increasingly overlap; and middle management got harder under AI-adoption expectations and stakeholder pressure. Orgs now hire for impact over title, at two extremes (deep specialists vs broadly capable engineers).
leadership_read.so_what: Useful for managers-of-managers thinking about retention and the IC-vs-management track conversation amid the layoff wave.
leadership_read.notes: Recent (2 Apr) but partly paywalled; the free portion may be too thin for the 400-600 word substance floor. Backup Leadership Read option.

in_practice.headline: Managing Managers: a director's short guide — schedule skip-levels with every IC, give new managers structure, and name the org dysfunctions
in_practice.source_name: Jay Vilalta (personal blog, director of engineering)
in_practice.summary: A first-person director's account of managing managers: he runs regular skip-levels with every IC (framed explicitly as support, not catching people out — 'I want to understand how we can better support you and your manager'), gives new managers heavy structure and frequent feedback because the IC-to-people-leader transition overwhelms them, and catalogues the recurring org dysfunctions he sees (unclear/shifting priorities, weak tooling, poor cross-team communication, misaligned incentives, thin planning). Lived and operational rather than normative.
in_practice.so_what: A transferable, named-operator model for the skip-level + new-manager-support core of manager-of-managers craft — exactly the season's theme, with concrete mechanics a reader can recognise without running the org.
in_practice.notes: QUALIFYING manager-of-managers In Practice piece: named operator (Jay Vilalta, director), journey/operational voice, implication-level. Sept 2025 = evergreen but within the spec's tolerance when fresh in-window material is thin (tag 'a classic worth revisiting'). Does NOT reuse the Issue 11 Monzo/Matias piece. Best In Practice candidate this season-slot.

in_practice.headline: Managing engineers more experienced than you — the three mistakes new managers make with senior ICs
in_practice.source_name: Diary of an Engineering Manager (Suresh Choudhary)
in_practice.summary: First-person EM account of three mistakes managing senior engineers: (1) trying to be the technical expert — instead acknowledge gaps and add process/coaching value; (2) getting completely out of the way — over-delegation backfires, so keep periodic check-ins and clear expectations; (3) withholding feedback — experienced engineers still need it, framed around broader impact. Core insight: the manager's value is creating clarity and multiplying impact, not technical superiority.
in_practice.so_what: Transferable coaching mechanics for EMs managing senior people — useful season material, though more EM-craft than strictly manager-of-managers.
in_practice.notes: Named operator, journey format — qualifies on the three In Practice qualities. Slightly off the manager-of-managers theme (it's EM-to-senior-IC). Backup if the Vilalta piece is rejected.

outside_in.headline: incident.io: how we measure on-call reliability from the customer's outcome, not the component
outside_in.summary: incident.io describes putting the customer at the centre of reliability: a 'good minutes / total minutes' SLO that ignores self-healing blips, 99.99% targets, dual telecom providers so a Twilio/APNs degradation isn't accepted as an excuse, and subtracting customer-configured delays from latency math. A clear 'here's how we think about reliability' shape with transferable lessons.
outside_in.so_what: A dev-tools/infra org's reliability philosophy that a banking/fintech leader can lift into their own SLO and on-call design — non-AI, non-banned company.
outside_in.notes: incident.io is NOT in the banned Outside In list (Monzo, Revolut, Ramp, Cloudflare, Wise, Linear). Sector = dev tools & infrastructure (rotation-friendly vs recent fintech/consumer picks). Non-AI subject — helps the long-form-variety rule. Strong Outside In candidate.

outside_in.headline: Figma's database journey: from one Postgres instance to ~100x growth and multi-terabyte tables
outside_in.summary: Figma engineering recounts scaling from a single Postgres instance on AWS's largest machine (2020) to ~100x growth four years later, with some tables at several terabytes and billions of rows — a 'we did X to get through Y' scale-transition narrative about how they sharded/scaled the database tier.
outside_in.so_what: A clean scale-transition story (single DB to sharded at scale) that maps onto any team facing the same growth wall — transferable architecture-leadership lesson.
outside_in.notes: Figma not in banned list; sector = enterprise/SaaS, good rotation. CAUTION: verify the specific post and its exact 2026 publish date before use — search surfaced the narrative but the canonical Figma sharding posts are older; may be a re-share. Treat date as needs-confirmation.

outside_in.headline: Duolingo: scaling engineering with agentic workflows — an honest 'still early, open questions' account
outside_in.summary: Duolingo engineering describes adopting agentic workflows, candidly noting it's 'still in the early days' and that both the capabilities and the best supporting infrastructure 'remain very much open questions' — a measured, non-hype peer account of one team's agent-adoption pattern.
outside_in.so_what: A grounded counterweight to vendor agent-adoption hype from a respected consumer-tech org — but it's AI-substrate, so using it would count against the long-form-variety cap.
outside_in.notes: Duolingo not banned; sector = consumer tech at scale. AI-substrate — if In Practice or Leadership Read is also AI, this would breach the v1.35 'at most one long-form AI' rule. Prefer the non-AI incident.io piece for Outside In. Date needs confirmation.

rabbit_hole.headline: Taming time in distributed systems — why there is no global clock, and how logical/vector clocks paper over it
rabbit_hole.summary: An accessible deep-dive into the oldest hard problem in distributed systems: machines have no shared clock and quartz drift (~1s every 11.6 days) means events can only be partially ordered. Walks through Lamport clocks (simple counters, can't detect concurrency) and vector clocks (N entries for N machines, can) as the standard workarounds.
rabbit_hole.so_what: A satisfying 'why is this so hard' explainer for the back-of-book curiosity slot — no action, pure understanding.
rabbit_hole.notes: Evergreen explainer surfaced this week; not date-critical. last_rabbit_hole_date was 2026-05-22 (used in Issue 11) so a rabbit hole is optional this issue. Captured as a low-priority option.
